quiz_questions.question_text: What type of lens does the human eye use?

options.text: A converging lens
A flat lens
A diverging lens
A prism

quiz_questions.explanation: The eye uses a converging lens to focus light on the retina. The lens shape is adjusted by ciliary muscles to focus on objects at different distances.

quiz_questions.question_text: What corrects myopia (nearsightedness)?

options.text: A mirror
Converging (convex) lens
A prism
Diverging (concave) lens

quiz_questions.explanation: Myopia (nearsightedness) is corrected with a diverging lens. The diverging lens spreads light before it enters the eye, moving the focal point back onto the retina.

quiz_questions.question_text: What corrects hyperopia (farsightedness)?

options.text: Diverging (concave) lens
A pinhole
A flat lens
Converging (convex) lens

quiz_questions.explanation: Hyperopia (farsightedness) is corrected with a converging lens that helps the eye focus light from nearby objects onto the retina.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the near point of a normal eye?

options.text: About 50 cm
About 1 m
About 25 cm
About 10 cm

quiz_questions.explanation: The near point is approximately 25 cm for a normal eye — the closest distance at which objects can be focused clearly. The far point is at infinity for normal vision.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a telescope, which lens has the larger focal length?

options.text: The objective lens
Both are equal
Neither — telescopes use mirrors only
The eyepiece

quiz_questions.explanation: In a refracting telescope, the objective lens has the larger focal length to collect light from distant objects, while the eyepiece has a shorter focal length to magnify the image.

quiz_questions.question_text: Where does the eye focus incoming light?

options.text: On the retina
On the pupil
On the cornea
On the iris

quiz_questions.explanation: The eye focuses incoming light onto the retina at the back of the eye. The retina contains photoreceptor cells that convert light into nerve signals sent to the brain.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the total magnification of a compound microscope?

options.text: M_objective / M_eyepiece
M_objective − M_eyepiece
M_objective + M_eyepiece
M_objective × M_eyepiece

quiz_questions.explanation: The total magnification of a compound microscope is the product of the objective magnification and the eyepiece magnification. For example, a 40× objective and a 10× eyepiece give 400× total magnification.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does a magnifying glass do when an object is closer than the focal point?

options.text: It creates a real inverted image on a screen
It produces a magnified, virtual, upright image
It makes the object appear smaller and farther away
It produces no image at all when used this way

quiz_questions.explanation: A magnifying glass (converging lens) produces a magnified, virtual, upright image when the object is placed between the lens and its focal point. This is the most common way a magnifying glass is used.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the far point of a normal eye?

options.text: About 10 meters away from the eye
About 25 centimeters from the eye
Infinity for a person with normal vision
About 100 meters away from the eye

quiz_questions.explanation: The far point of a normal eye is at infinity, meaning the eye can focus on very distant objects. A person with myopia has a far point closer than infinity.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does a telescope do?

options.text: It makes very small nearby objects appear much larger
It makes distant objects appear closer and more detailed
It corrects vision problems like myopia or hyperopia
It splits white light into a spectrum of different colors

quiz_questions.explanation: A telescope uses a combination of lenses (or mirrors) to make distant objects appear closer and more detailed. The objective collects light and the eyepiece magnifies the resulting image.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'myopia' in terms of eye optics?

options.text: A condition where the eye focuses distant images in front of the retina
A condition where the eye focuses nearby images behind the retina
A condition where the eye cannot distinguish different colors at all
A condition where the eye lens becomes completely opaque over time

quiz_questions.explanation: Myopia (nearsightedness) occurs when the eyeball is too long or the lens is too powerful, causing images of distant objects to focus in front of the retina instead of on it.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'hyperopia' in terms of eye optics?

options.text: A condition where distant objects appear blurry to the observer
A condition where nearby objects focus behind the retina surface
A condition caused by uneven curvature of the cornea surface
A condition where both near and far objects appear equally blurry

quiz_questions.explanation: Hyperopia (farsightedness) occurs when the eyeball is too short or the lens is too weak, causing images of nearby objects to focus behind the retina. Distant objects may still be clear.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'retina' and what is its function?

options.text: The colored part of the eye that controls how much light enters
The light-sensitive layer at the back of the eye that detects images
The transparent front covering of the eye that refracts light first
The adjustable opening in the eye that changes size in bright light

quiz_questions.explanation: The retina is the light-sensitive layer at the back of the eye. It contains photoreceptor cells (rods and cones) that convert focused light into electrical signals sent to the brain via the optic nerve.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'objective lens' in a microscope or telescope?

options.text: The lens closest to the observer's eye for magnifying the image
The lens closest to the object being observed that gathers light
A corrective lens used to fix chromatic aberration in the system
A flat mirror used to redirect light into the optical instrument

quiz_questions.explanation: The objective lens is the lens closest to the object being viewed. In a telescope, it has a long focal length to collect light from distant objects. In a microscope, it has a short focal length for high magnification.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'eyepiece' of an optical instrument?

options.text: The lens closest to the object that forms the initial image
The lens closest to the eye that magnifies the image further
The protective cover that shields the instrument from damage
The focusing knob that adjusts the distance between the lenses

quiz_questions.explanation: The eyepiece is the lens closest to the observer's eye. It acts like a magnifying glass, further enlarging the image produced by the objective lens.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'accommodation' mean for the human eye?

options.text: The ability of the pupil to change size in response to light levels
The ability of the lens to change shape to focus at different distances
The ability of the retina to adjust its sensitivity to different colors
The ability of the cornea to filter out harmful ultraviolet radiation

quiz_questions.explanation: Accommodation is the process by which the ciliary muscles change the shape of the eye's lens to focus on objects at different distances. The lens becomes rounder for nearby objects and flatter for distant ones.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'angular magnification' mean for a telescope?

options.text: How much closer the object physically moves toward the observer
How much larger the angle subtended by the image is compared to the object
How much brighter the image appears compared to the naked eye view
How many degrees the telescope can rotate to scan the entire sky

quiz_questions.explanation: Angular magnification is the ratio of the angle subtended at the eye by the image through the instrument to the angle subtended by the object viewed with the naked eye. For a telescope, M = f_objective / f_eyepiece.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'cornea' of the eye?

options.text: The opening that controls how much light enters the eye interior
The transparent front surface that does most of the light refraction
The layer at the back of the eye that detects the focused image
The colored ring that gives the eye its distinctive color appearance

quiz_questions.explanation: The cornea is the transparent, curved front surface of the eye. It performs about two-thirds of the eye's total refraction, bending incoming light before it reaches the adjustable lens behind it.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'compound microscope'?

options.text: A microscope using a single magnifying lens to view small objects
A microscope using two lens systems for higher total magnification
A microscope that uses electron beams instead of visible light
A microscope that uses mirrors instead of any lenses for magnification

quiz_questions.explanation: A compound microscope uses two lens systems — the objective and the eyepiece — to achieve high total magnification. The total magnification is the product of both individual magnifications.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'near point' of the eye?

options.text: The farthest distance at which the eye can focus on an object clearly
The closest distance at which the eye can focus on an object clearly
The distance from the lens to the retina inside the eyeball itself
The distance from the cornea to the center of the eye's lens

quiz_questions.explanation: The near point is the closest distance at which the eye can focus clearly on an object. For a normal eye, this is approximately 25 cm. The near point increases with age.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student with myopia cannot read the board from the back of the classroom. Which type of corrective lens should they wear?

options.text: A converging lens to bring the image forward onto the retina
A diverging lens to move the image back onto the retina surface
A cylindrical lens to correct for an unevenly shaped cornea
A bifocal lens that has both converging and diverging sections

quiz_questions.explanation: Myopia causes distant images to focus in front of the retina. A diverging (concave) lens spreads the light slightly before it enters the eye, effectively moving the focal point back onto the retina.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student uses a microscope with a 10× eyepiece and a 40× objective. What is the total magnification?

options.text: Total magnification is 50× (addition of both values)
Total magnification is 400× (product of both values)
Total magnification is 30× (difference of both values)
Total magnification is 4× (ratio of the two values)

quiz_questions.explanation: In a compound microscope, the total magnification equals the objective magnification multiplied by the eyepiece magnification: 40× × 10× = 400×.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student looks through a magnifying glass and sees an upright, enlarged image of an insect. Where must the insect be placed?

options.text: The insect must be placed beyond twice the focal length of the lens
The insect must be placed between the lens and its focal point
The insect must be placed exactly at the focal point of the lens
The insect must be placed on the opposite side of the lens from the eye

quiz_questions.explanation: A magnifying glass (converging lens) produces a virtual, upright, magnified image only when the object is placed between the lens and its focal point. This is the standard way to use a magnifying glass.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student builds a simple refracting telescope. To increase magnification, what should they change?

options.text: Use a longer focal length eyepiece to increase the magnification ratio
Use a shorter focal length eyepiece to increase the magnification ratio
Make the telescope tube shorter to bring the lenses closer together
Use two identical lenses with the same focal length for both parts

quiz_questions.explanation: Telescope magnification = f_objective / f_eyepiece. To increase magnification, the student should use an eyepiece with a shorter focal length, which increases the ratio.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a lab, a student notices their microscope image is blurry. They adjust the focus knob. What is being changed physically?

options.text: The brightness of the light source illuminating the sample slide
The magnification power of the eyepiece lens in the microscope
The distance between the objective lens and the specimen on the slide
The wavelength of light passing through the lenses of the microscope

quiz_questions.explanation: The focus knob adjusts the distance between the objective lens and the specimen. This changes the object distance, which must be precisely set for the lens equation to produce a clear, focused image.

quiz_questions.question_text: An older person has trouble reading a book held at 25 cm. A doctor prescribes converging lenses. What condition do they have?

options.text: Myopia, where distant objects are blurry and need diverging lenses
Hyperopia or presbyopia, where near objects need converging lenses
Astigmatism, where all objects are distorted at every distance
Color blindness, which cannot be corrected with any type of lens

quiz_questions.explanation: Difficulty seeing nearby objects is hyperopia or presbyopia (age-related farsightedness). Converging lenses help by adding extra refracting power to focus nearby images onto the retina.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student observes cells through a microscope. The image appears upside down and backwards. Why?

options.text: The microscope's mirror reflects the image in a reversed orientation
The objective lens forms a real inverted image that the eyepiece magnifies
The slide was accidentally placed upside down on the microscope stage
The eyepiece lens inverts the image a second time during viewing

quiz_questions.explanation: The objective lens in a compound microscope forms a real, inverted image of the specimen. The eyepiece then magnifies this already-inverted image, so the final view appears upside down and reversed.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student looks at the Moon through a telescope and sees a much larger image. What type of image is being formed by the eyepiece?

options.text: A real, diminished image projected inside the telescope tube
A virtual, magnified image that the eye perceives through the lens
A real, magnified image that could be projected onto a screen
A virtual, diminished image that makes the Moon look farther away

quiz_questions.explanation: The eyepiece of a telescope acts like a magnifying glass, producing a virtual, magnified image of the real image formed by the objective lens. This virtual image is what the observer's eye sees.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student learns that cameras use converging lenses. Where does the image form in a camera?

options.text: On the film or sensor behind the lens as a real inverted image
In front of the lens as a virtual upright image of the scene
Inside the lens itself between the two curved glass surfaces
At the focal point of the lens regardless of the object distance

quiz_questions.explanation: A camera lens (converging) forms a real, inverted, diminished image on the film or digital sensor behind the lens. The sensor records this image, and software flips it right-side up for display.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student wonders why astronomers prefer reflecting telescopes (mirrors) over refracting telescopes (lenses) for large instruments. What is a key reason?

options.text: Mirrors cannot form images of stars, only lenses can do that properly
Large mirrors are easier to support and do not suffer from chromatic aberration
Reflecting telescopes have lower magnification than refracting telescopes
Refracting telescopes produce brighter images than reflecting telescopes do

quiz_questions.explanation: Large mirrors can be supported from behind, while large lenses sag under their own weight. Mirrors also avoid chromatic aberration (color fringing) that affects lenses, making reflecting telescopes preferred for professional astronomy.
